Method: The authors developed and applied a user onboarding improvement framework to the Research Infrastructure Services at Washington University in St. Louis, incorporating empirical validation.

Result: The framework was successfully implemented and evaluated, improving the onboarding experience for new users at the institution.

Conclusion: The study highlights the significance of simplified onboarding processes and validates the proposed framework as effective in easing user confusion and improving system accessibility.

Abstract: Research computing centers around the world struggle with onboarding new users. Subject matter experts, researchers, and principal investigators are often overwhelmed by the complex infrastructure and software offerings designed to support diverse research domains at large academic and national institutions. As a result, users frequently fall into confusion and complexity to access these resources, despite the availability of documentation, tutorials, interactive trainings and other similar resources. Through this work, we present a framework designed to improve new-user onboarding experience. We also present an empirical validation through its application within the Research Infrastructure Services at Washington University in St. Louis.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [355] [The Effect of Idea Elaboration on the Automatic Assessment of Idea Originality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20569)
*Umberto Domanti,Moritz Mock,Sergio Agnoli,Antonella De Angeli*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper examines how Large Language Models (LLMs) align with human raters in originality assessment and confirms a self-preference bias in LLMs, favoring artificial responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address key limitations of human originality assessment (cost, fatigue, and subjectivity) using automatic systems, while investigating potential biases like self-preference in LLMs.

Method: The authors analyzed 4,813 responses to a divergent thinking task, comparing ratings from two human judges, two fine-tuned automatic systems, and ChatGPT-4o, all with standardized instructions.

Result: Findings revealed a self-preference bias in LLMs, with a tendency to favor machine-generated responses, though this bias disappeared when controlling for idea elaboration.

Conclusion: While LLMs show potential for assessing originality, self-preference bias needs further consideration, and future research should address its implications in creativity assessment.

Abstract: Automatic systems are increasingly used to assess the originality of responses in creative tasks. They offer a potential solution to key limitations of human assessment (cost, fatigue, and subjectivity), but there is preliminary evidence of a self-preference bias. Accordingly, automatic systems tend to prefer outcomes that are more closely related to their style, rather than to the human one. In this paper, we investigated how Large Language Models (LLMs) align with human raters in assessing the originality of responses in a divergent thinking task. We analysed 4,813 responses to the Alternate Uses Task produced by higher and lower creative humans and ChatGPT-4o. Human raters were two university students who underwent intensive training. Machine raters were two specialised systems fine-tuned on AUT responses and corresponding human ratings (OCSAI and CLAUS) and ChatGPT-4o, which was prompted with the same instructions as human raters. Results confirmed the presence of a self-preference bias in LLMs. Automatic systems tended to privilege artificial responses. However, this self-preference bias disappeared when the analyses controlled for the idea elaboration. We discuss theoretical and methodological implications of these findings by highlighting future directions for research on creativity assessment.

</details>


<div id='math.DS'></div>

# math.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [356] [Spectral Kernel Dynamics for Planetary Surface Graphs: Distinction Dynamics and Topological Conservation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20887)
*Jnaneshwar Das*

Main category: math.DS

TL;DR: This paper analyzes a spectral kernel field equation, addresses conservation-law violations, and provides methods to remedy these issues, including mathematical derivations and practical diagnostics for planetary networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of conservation-law properties in the spectral kernel field equation and develop methods to mitigate these conservation deficits.

Method: The paper proves analytical properties like volume-expansion of fixed-point flows and connects stability margins to conservation deficits. It introduces the distinction dynamics equation and derives the conditional topology-preserving compression theorem on 3D surface graphs.

Result: The authors propose a triple spectral diagnostic system for planetary drainage networks and provide analytical and worked-out examples, demonstrating when conservation assumptions may fail.

Conclusion: The study advances understanding of spectral kernel conservation challenges, offering preliminary diagnostics and laying the groundwork for further research, though some extensions remain future work.

Abstract: The spectral kernel field equation R[k] = T[k] lacks a conservation-law analog. We prove (i) the fixed-point flow is strictly volume-expanding (tr DF > 0), precluding automatic conservation, and (ii) the conservation deficit per mode equals the Hessian stability margin exactly: D_m = -Delta'. Closing the deficit requires a scene-side compensating contribution, which we formalise as the distinction dynamics equation dc/dt = G[c, h_t], with MaxCal-optimal realisation G_opt. On fixed-topology 3D surface graphs we derive a conditional topology-preserving compression theorem: retaining k >= beta_0 + beta_1 modes (under a spectral-ordering assumption) preserves all Betti-number charges; we include a worked short-cycle counterexample (figure-eight) calibrating when the assumption fails. A triple necessary spectral diagnostic -- Fiedler-mode concentration, elevated curl energy, anomalous beta_1 -- is derived for planetary drainage networks at O(N) cost. Two internal real-data sequences serve as preliminary consistency checks; full benchmarks and adaptive-topology extensions are deferred.

</details>


<div id='quant-ph'></div>

# quant-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [357] [Suppressing the Erasure Error of Fusion Operation in Photonic Quantum Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.21475)
*Xiangyu Ren,Yuexun Huang,Zhemin Zhang,Yuchen Zhu,Tsung-Yi Ho,Antonio Barbalace,Zhiding Liang*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces a tree-encoded fusion strategy for mitigating fusion erasure errors in photonic quantum computing and develops a compiler framework that achieves improved robustness and efficiency compared to prior work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the shortcomings of existing MBQC compilation, particularly the failure to account for fusion erasure errors, which are more damaging and limit reliable photonic quantum computing executions.

Method: The authors propose tree-encoded fusion to suppress erasure errors during graph-state generation and integrate this into a compiler framework with optimized algorithms to reduce execution overhead.

Result: The proposed framework demonstrated robustness, exponential improvement in execution efficiency compared to OneAdapt, and feasibility through a demonstration on real PQC hardware.

Conclusion: Tree-encoded fusion and its associated compiler framework substantially advance the reliability and efficiency of photonic quantum computing, specifically addressing fusion erasure errors overlooked by previous approaches.

Abstract: Photonic quantum computing provides a promising route toward quantum computation by naturally supporting the measurement-based quantum computation (MBQC) model. In MBQC, programs are executed through measurements on a pre-generated graph state, whose construction largely depends on probabilistic fusion operations. However, fusion operations in PQC are vulnerable to two major error sources: fusion failure and fusion erasure. As a result, MBQC compilation must account for both error mechanisms to generate reliable and efficient photonic executions. Prior state-of-the-art MBQC compilation, represented by OneAdapt, is designed for all-photonic architectures and mainly focuses on handling fusion failures. Nevertheless, it does not explicitly model fusion erasures induced by photon loss, which can be substantially more damaging than fusion failures.
  To mitigate fusion erasure errors, we introduce a new MBQC compilation scheme built upon the spin qubit quantum memory. We propose tree-encoded fusion, an encoding strategy that suppresses erasure errors during graph-state generation. We further incorporate this scheme into a compiler framework with algorithms that reduce the execution overhead of quantum programs. We evaluate the proposed framework using a realistic PQC simulator on six representative quantum algorithm benchmarks across multiple program scales. The results show that tree-encoded fusion achieves better robustness than alternative fusion-encoding strategies, and that our compiler provides exponential improvement over OneAdapt. In addition, we validate the feasibility of our approach through a proof-of-concept demonstration on real PQC hardware.

</details>


### [358] [Quantum-HPC Software Stacks and the openQSE Reference Architecture: A Survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20912)
*Amir Shehata,Brian Austin,Tom Beck,Lukas Burgholzer,Alex Chernoguzov,Spencer Churchill,Andrea Delgado,Yasuko Eckert,Jeffery Heckey,Kevin Kissell,Katherine Klymko,Josh Moles,Thomas Naughton,Lee James O'Riordan,Christian Ortiz Pauyac,Guen Prawiroatmodjo,Ermal Rrapaj,Jiri Schindler,Laura Schulz,Sebastian Stern,Tyler Takeshita,Miwako Tsuji,Aleksander Wennersteen,Travis Humble,Martin Schulz*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper discusses the isolation and proprietary nature of current quantum high-performance computing (QHPC) stacks, identifies their common challenges and requirements, and proposes the openQSE architecture to standardize and integrate QHPC systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the lack of standardized interfaces and interoperability in QHPC software stacks, which hampers integration with HPC and cloud environments.

Method: Analyzed nine production QHPC stacks to determine common design patterns, challenges, and requirements, and proposed the openQSE reference architecture to standardize layers for better interoperability.

Result: Identified consistent needs in runtime abstraction, resource management, interconnect semantics, and observability. Proposed the openQSE architecture to unify QHPC software ecosystems.

Conclusion: openQSE offers a structured, flexible, and future-proof reference architecture enabling interoperability in QHPC, serving both NISQ and FTQC workloads effectively.

Abstract: Quantum resources are increasingly integrated into high-performance computing (HPC) and cloud environments, but quantum high-performance computing (QHPC) software stacks remain isolated, often proprietary, full-stack solutions lacking common interfaces across runtime, resource management, orchestration, and execution layers. This paper analyzes nine production QHPC stacks and identifies common design patterns and emerging requirements, covering deployment models, application interaction patterns, SDK support, and readiness for fault-tolerant operation. The survey exposes consistent needs in runtime abstraction, resource management, interconnect semantics, and observability. Based on these findings, we propose the open quantum-HPC software ecosystem ( openQSE) reference architecture as a first step toward unifying the state-of-the-practice. openQSE defines a set of layer boundaries that allow different implementations to interoperate while preserving deployment flexibility, and is structured to support both current noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) workloads and future fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC) systems without changes to upper-layer application interfaces.

</details>


<div id='eess.SY'></div>

# eess.SY [[Back]](#toc)

### [359] [A Systematic Review and Taxonomy of Reinforcement Learning-Model Predictive Control Integration for Linear Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.21030)
*Mohsen Jalaeian Farimani,Roya Khalili Amirabadi,Davoud Nikkhouy,Malihe Abdolbaghi,Mahshad Rastegarmoghaddam,Shima Samadzadeh*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: This paper conducts a systematic literature review (SLR) of RL-MPC integrations for linear systems, offering a taxonomy and cross-dimensional synthesis that highlights trends, strategies, and challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the fragmented literature on RL-MPC integration for linear systems by providing a structured overview and identifying methodological trends.

Method: A systematic literature review, creating a multi-dimensional taxonomy for classifying RL functional roles, RL algorithms, MPC formulations, cost structures, and application domains.

Result: Insights into trends, integration strategies, practical challenges such as computational burden and robustness, and closed-loop guarantees in RL-MPC architectures.

Conclusion: The synthesis offers researchers and practitioners a valuable reference for designing RL-MPC systems using linear predictive models.

Abstract: The integration of Model Predictive Control (MPC) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for constrained decision-making and adaptive control. MPC offers structured optimization, explicit constraint handling, and established stability tools, whereas RL provides data-driven adaptation and performance improvement in the presence of uncertainty and model mismatch. Despite the rapid growth of research on RL--MPC integration, the literature remains fragmented, particularly for control architectures built on linear or linearized predictive models. This paper presents a comprehensive Systematic Literature Review (SLR) of RL--MPC integrations for linear and linearized systems, covering peer-reviewed and formally indexed studies published until 2025. The reviewed studies are organized through a multi-dimensional taxonomy covering RL functional roles, RL algorithm classes, MPC formulations, cost-function structures, and application domains. In addition, a cross-dimensional synthesis is conducted to identify recurring design patterns and reported associations among these dimensions within the reviewed corpus. The review highlights methodological trends, commonly adopted integration strategies, and recurring practical challenges, including computational burden, sample efficiency, robustness, and closed-loop guarantees. The resulting synthesis provides a structured reference for researchers and practitioners seeking to design or analyze RL--MPC architectures based on linear or linearized predictive control formulations.

</details>


### [360] [A Case Study in Recovery of Drones using Discrete-Event Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.21740)
*Liam P. Burns,Dayse M. Cavalcanti,Felipe G. Cabral,Max H. de Queiroz,Melissa Greeff,Publio M. M. Lima,Karen Rudie*

Main category: eess.SY

TL;DR: The paper explores a novel hybrid architecture for swarm robotics recovery combining discrete-event systems and continuous controllers, implemented on simulated drones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in applying discrete-event systems to swarm robotics, particularly for recovery methods in fault or attack events.

Method: A hybrid system integrating a discrete-event systems supervisor with a continuous controller for fault recovery in swarm robotics scenarios. Recovery performance is demonstrated through simulations.

Result: The approach was tested with ten UAVs in simulations, showcasing recovery capabilities across four scenarios, with a secondary supervisor for drone regrouping after re-entering operational zones.

Conclusion: Implementing discrete-event systems in swarm robotics demonstrates effective recovery capabilities, suggesting promising applications for fault management in multi-drone settings.

Abstract: Discrete-event systems and supervisory control theory provide a rigorous framework for specifying correct-by-construction behavior. However, their practical application to swarm robotics remains largely underexplored. In this paper, we investigate a topological recovery method based on discrete-event-systems within a swarm robotics context. We propose a hybrid architecture that combines a high-level discrete event systems supervisor with a low-level continuous controller, allowing lost drones to safely recover from fault or attack events and re-enter a controlled region. The method is demonstrated using ten simulated UAVs in the py-bullet-drones framework. We show recovery performance across four distinct scenarios, each with varying initial state estimates. Additionally, we introduce a secondary recovery supervisor that manages the regrouping process for a drone after it has re-entered the operational region.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [361] [AtomicRAG: Atom-Entity Graphs for Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20844)
*Yanning Hou,Duanyang Yuan,Sihang Zhou,Xiaoshu Chen,Ke Liang,Siwei Wang,Xinwang Liu,Jian Huang*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces AtomicRAG, a novel retrieval method using an Atom-Entity Graph for better knowledge representation and reasoning, outperforming prior GraphRAG baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous GraphRAG methods were limited by rigidly grouping multiple atomic facts into text chunks and errors in triple-based entity linking, leading to reduced retrieval accuracy and flexibility.

Method: The method involves representing knowledge as individual 'knowledge atoms' rather than coarse-grained text chunks, and establishing simpler edge relationships between entities using personalized PageRank and relevance-based filtering for more reliable indexing and retrieval.

Result: The proposed AtomicRAG algorithm achieves superior retrieval accuracy and robustness in reasoning compared to existing RAG baselines, as demonstrated through experiments on five public benchmarks.

Conclusion: AtomicRAG improves the flexibility, precision, and robustness of knowledge representation and retrieval systems by focusing on atomic knowledge units and refining entity connections.

Abstract: Recent GraphRAG methods integrate graph structures into text indexing and retrieval, using knowledge graph triples to connect text chunks, thereby improving retrieval coverage and precision. However, we observe that treating text chunks as the basic unit of knowledge representation rigidly groups multiple atomic facts together, limiting the flexibility and adaptability needed to support diverse retrieval scenarios. Additionally, triple-based entity linking is sensitive to relation-extraction errors, which can lead to missing or incorrect reasoning paths and ultimately hurt retrieval accuracy. To address these issues, we propose the Atom-Entity Graph, a more precise and reliable architecture for knowledge representation and indexing. In our approach, knowledge is stored as knowledge atoms, namely individual, self-contained units of factual information, rather than coarse-grained text chunks. This allows knowledge elements to be flexibly reassembled without mutual interference, thereby enabling seamless alignment with diverse query perspectives. Edges between entities simply indicate whether a relationship exists. By combining personalized PageRank with relevance-based filtering, we maintain accurate entity connections and improve the reliability of reasoning. Theoretical analysis and experiments on five public benchmarks show that the proposed AtomicRAG algorithm outperforms strong RAG baselines in retrieval accuracy and reasoning robustness. Code: https://github.com/7HHHHH/AtomicRAG.

</details>


### [362] [CaST-POI: Candidate-Conditioned Spatiotemporal Modeling for Next POI Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20845)
*Zhenyu Yu,Chunlei Meng,Yangchen Zeng,Mohd Yamani Idna Idris,Shuigeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: CaST-POI is a candidate-conditioned spatiotemporal model for predicting users' next points-of-interest, outperforming existing methods in accuracy and scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve next POI recommendation by addressing the limitation of existing methods that evaluate candidate POIs uniformly without considering historical visit relevance based on the candidate.

Method: The authors introduce a candidate-conditioned sequence reader and apply candidate-relative temporal and spatial biases to refine predictions by dynamically attending to user history based on individual candidate POIs.

Result: CaST-POI demonstrates superior performance over state-of-the-art methods across multiple metrics and excels particularly well under scenarios with large candidate pools.

Conclusion: CaST-POI offers a significant advancement in POI recommendation by leveraging candidate-specific relevance and dynamic user history interpretation, contributing to better scalability and accuracy.

Abstract: Next Point-of-Interest (POI) recommendation plays a crucial role in location-based services by predicting users' future mobility patterns. Existing methods typically compute a single user representation from historical trajectories and use it to score all candidate POIs uniformly. However, this candidate-agnostic paradigm overlooks that the relevance of historical visits inherently depends on which candidate is being evaluated. In this paper, we propose CaST-POI, a candidate-conditioned spatiotemporal model for next POI recommendation. Our key insight is that the same user history should be interpreted differently when evaluating different candidate POIs. CaST-POI employs a candidate-conditioned sequence reader that uses candidates as queries to dynamically attend to user history. In addition, we introduce candidate-relative temporal and spatial biases to capture fine-grained mobility patterns based on the relationships between historical visits and each candidate POI. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that CaST-POI consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, yielding substantial improvements across multiple evaluation metrics, with particularly strong advantages under large candidate pools. Code is available at https://github.com/YuZhenyuLindy/CaST-POI.git.

</details>


### [363] [ADS-POI: Agentic Spatiotemporal State Decomposition for Next Point-of-Interest Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20846)
*Zhenyu Yu,Chunlei Meng,Yangchen Zeng,Mohd Yamani Idna Idris,Shuigeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The authors introduce ADS-POI, a framework for next POI recommendation that refines user behavior modeling by decomposing it into multiple spatiotemporal sub-states, improving prediction effectiveness and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods in next POI recommendation struggle with limitations due to the entanglement of heterogeneous behavioral signals in a single latent representation. This reduces flexibility and adaptability to diverse contexts.

Method: ADS-POI employs multiple latent sub-states with distinct spatiotemporal transition dynamics, which are selectively combined via a context-aware mechanism to form the state used for prediction.

Result: ADS-POI significantly outperforms prior methods across three real-world datasets, demonstrating enhanced predictive accuracy and robustness for POI recommendation.

Conclusion: Decomposing user behavior into coordinated spatiotemporal sub-states allows ADS-POI to adapt effectively to varying behavioral factors, making it superior in POI prediction tasks.

Abstract: Next point-of-interest (POI) recommendation requires modeling user mobility as a spatiotemporal sequence, where different behavioral factors may evolve at different temporal and spatial scales. Most existing methods compress a user's history into a single latent representation, which tends to entangle heterogeneous signals such as routine mobility patterns, short-term intent, and temporal regularities. This entanglement limits the flexibility of state evolution and reduces the model's ability to adapt to diverse decision contexts. We propose ADS-POI, a spatiotemporal state decomposition framework for next POI recommendation. ADS-POI represents a user with multiple parallel evolving latent sub-states, each governed by its own spatiotemporal transition dynamics. These sub-states are selectively aggregated through a context-conditioned mechanism to form the decision state used for prediction. This design enables different behavioral components to evolve at different rates while remaining coordinated under the current spatiotemporal context. Extensive experiments on three real-world benchmark datasets from Foursquare and Gowalla demonstrate that ADS-POI consistently outperforms strong state-of-the-art baselines under a full-ranking evaluation protocol. The results show that decomposing user behavior into multiple spatiotemporally aware states leads to more effective and robust next POI recommendation. Our code is available at https://github.com/YuZhenyuLindy/ADS-POI.git.

</details>


### [364] [Revisiting Content-Based Music Recommendation: Efficient Feature Aggregation from Large-Scale Music Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20847)
*Yizhi Zhou,Jia-Qi Yang,De-Chuan Zhan,Da-Wei Zhou*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper highlights the drawbacks of current Music Recommendation Systems (MRSs) and introduces TASTE, a multimodal dataset and benchmarking framework for improving music recommendations using content-driven approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches to MRSs rely heavily on collaborative filtering, which often ignores audio characteristics and performs poorly in cold-start scenarios. Additionally, current datasets and evaluation frameworks lack the integration of multimodal information and fail to support diverse algorithms.

Method: The authors introduce TASTE, a dataset and benchmarking framework that integrates audio and textual modalities. They deploy large-scale self-supervised music encoders for feature extraction and propose MuQ-token as a novel method for efficient integration of multi-layer audio features.

Result: Experimental results show that leveraging audio representations and using the MuQ-token method enhance recommendation performance across tasks such as candidate recall and click-through rate (CTR).

Conclusion: The study validates the importance of multimodal information for effective music recommendations, offering a reusable framework (TASTE) and setting a new foundation for research in multimodal recommender systems.

Abstract: Music Recommendation Systems (MRSs) are a cornerstone of modern streaming platforms. Existing recommendation models, spanning both recall and ranking stages, predominantly rely on collaborative filtering, which fails to exploit the intrinsic characteristics of audio and consequently leads to suboptimal performance, particularly in cold-start scenarios. However, existing music recommendation datasets often lack rich multimodal information, such as raw audio signals and descriptive textual metadata. Moreover, current recommender system evaluation frameworks remain inadequate, as they neither fully leverage multimodal information nor support a diverse range of algorithms, especially multimodal methods. To address these limitations, we propose TASTE, a comprehensive dataset and benchmarking framework designed to highlight the role of multimodal information in music recommendation. Our dataset integrates both audio and textual modalities. By leveraging recent large-scale self-supervised music encoders, we demonstrate the substantial value of the extracted audio representations across recommendation tasks, including candidate recall and CTR. In addition, we introduce the \textbf{MuQ-token} method, which enables more efficient integration of multi-layer audio features. This method consistently outperforms other feature integration techniques across various settings. Overall, our results not only validate the effectiveness of content-driven approaches but also provide a highly effective and reusable multimodal foundation for future research. Code is available at https://github.com/zreach/TASTE

</details>


### [365] [MATRAG: Multi-Agent Transparent Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Explainable Recommendations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20848)
*Sushant Mehta*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces MATRAG, a novel framework integrating agents and knowledge graph-based retrieval for explainable recommendation systems, achieving high performance and trustworthiness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based recommendation systems lack transparency, grounding in knowledge, and coherent user-friendly explanations, limiting user trust.

Method: MATRAG employs a multi-agent framework with dynamic user modeling, semantic item analysis, collaborative reasoning, and natural language explanation generation. It includes a transparency scoring mechanism and combines retrieved knowledge.

Result: MATRAG significantly improves recommendation accuracy by 12.7% (Hit Rate) and 15.3% (NDCG) compared to baselines. 87.4% of explanations were rated as helpful by domain experts.

Conclusion: MATRAG advances explainable and transparent recommendation system technology with practical insights for production deployment.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based recommendation systems have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in understanding user preferences and generating personalized suggestions. However, existing approaches face critical challenges in transparency, knowledge grounding, and the ability to provide coherent explanations that foster user trust. We introduce MATRAG (Multi-Agent Transparent Retrieval-Augmented Generation), a novel framework that combined multi-agent collaboration with knowledge graph-augmented retrieval to deliver explainable recommendations. MATRAG employs four specialized agents: a User Modeling Agent that constructs dynamic preference profiles, an Item Analysis Agent that extracts semantic features from knowledge graphs, a Reasoning Agent that synthesizes collaborative and content-based signals, and an Explanation Agent that generates natural language justifications grounded in retrieved knowledge. Our framework incorporates a transparency scoring mechanism that quantifies explanation faithfulness and relevance. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets (Amazon Reviews, MovieLens-1M, and Yelp) demonstrate that MATRAG achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving recommendation accuracy by 12.7\% (Hit Rate) and 15.3\% (NDCG) over leading baselines, while human evaluation confirms that 87.4\% of generated explanations are rated as helpful and trustworthy by domain experts. Our work establishes new benchmarks for transparent, agentic recommendation systems and provides actionable insights for deploying LLM-based recommenders in production environments.

</details>


### [366] [SPIRE: Structure-Preserving Interpretable Retrieval of Evidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20849)
*Mike Rainey,Umut Acar,Muhammed Sezer*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a structure-aware retrieval system for semi-structured sources like HTML, improving efficacy over traditional flat passage-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing retrieval-augmented generation systems struggle with structured documents like HTML due to their linear document representation, leading to challenges in providing compact yet contextually-rich evidence.

Method: The authors propose a retrieval pipeline using tree-structured subdocuments. This includes defining document primitives (paths, pruning, and contextualization), embedding-based candidate generation, document-aware query aggregation, and contextual filtering.

Result: Experiments on HTML question-answering datasets show improved quality, diversity, and scalability of citations under fixed resource budgets when compared to traditional passage-based methods.

Conclusion: Preserving structural information and selectively contextualizing subdocuments offers significant benefits in semi-structured document retrieval systems.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation over semi-structured sources such as HTML is constrained by a mismatch between document structure and the flat, sequence-based interfaces of today's embedding and generative models. Retrieval pipelines often linearize documents into fixed-size chunks before indexing, which obscures section structure, lists, and tables, and makes it difficult to return small, citation-ready evidence without losing the surrounding context that makes it interpretable.
  We present a structure-aware retrieval pipeline that operates over tree-structured documents. The core idea is to represent candidates as subdocuments: precise, addressable selections that preserve structural identity while deferring the choice of surrounding context. We define a small set of document primitives--paths and path sets, subdocument extraction by pruning, and two contextualization mechanisms. Global contextualization adds the non-local scaffolding needed to make a selection intelligible (e.g., titles, headers, list and table structure). Local contextualization expands a seed selection within its structural neighborhood to obtain a compact, context-rich view under a target budget. Building on these primitives, we describe an embedding-based candidate generator that indexes sentence-seeded subdocuments and a query-time, document-aware aggregation step that amortizes shared structural context. We then introduce a contextual filtering stage that re-scores retrieved candidates using locally contextualized views.
  Across experiments on HTML question-answering benchmarks, we find that preserving structure while contextualizing selections yields higher-quality, more diverse citations under fixed budgets than strong passage-based baselines, while maintaining scalability.

</details>


### [367] [Association Is Not Similarity: Learning Corpus-Specific Associations for Multi-Hop Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20850)
*Jason Dury*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Association-Augmented Retrieval (AAR), a transductive reranking method designed to improve dense passage retrieval for multi-hop questions by leveraging passage co-occurrence associations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current dense retrieval methods struggle with multi-hop questions due to the need for associative reasoning between passages, which is not adequately captured by embedding similarity alone.

Method: AAR employs a small MLP trained using contrastive learning on passage co-occurrence annotations. At inference, it reranks passage candidates from dense retrieval using bi-directional association scoring.

Result: On HotpotQA, AAR improves passage Recall@5 from 0.831 to 0.916 (+8.6 points). Gains are particularly high in hard cases (+28.5 points). On MuSiQue, transductive evaluation shows a +10.1 point improvement, while inductive models do not show significant benefits due to corpus-specific patterns. Downstream QA benefits include +6.4 exact match improvement.

Conclusion: AAR effectively enhances multi-hop retrieval in dense systems by leveraging corpus-specific associative relationships, proving lightweight, fast to train, and impactful for QA tasks without the need for large language models (LLMs).

Abstract: Dense retrieval systems rank passages by embedding similarity to a query, but multi-hop questions require passages that are associatively related through shared reasoning chains. We introduce Association-Augmented Retrieval (AAR), a lightweight transductive reranking method that trains a small MLP (4.2M parameters) to learn associative relationships between passages in embedding space using contrastive learning on co-occurrence annotations. At inference time, AAR reranks an initial dense retrieval candidate set using bi-directional association scoring. On HotpotQA, AAR improves passage Recall@5 from 0.831 to 0.916 (+8.6 points) without evaluation-set tuning, with gains concentrated on hard questions where the dense baseline fails (+28.5 points). On MuSiQue, AAR achieves +10.1 points in the transductive setting. An inductive model trained on training-split associations and evaluated on unseen validation associations shows no significant improvement, suggesting that the method captures corpus-specific co-occurrences rather than transferable patterns. Ablation studies support this interpretation: training on semantically similar but non-associated passage pairs degrades retrieval below the baseline, while shuffling association pairs causes severe degradation. A downstream QA evaluation shows retrieval gains translate to +6.4 exact match improvement. The method adds 3.7ms per query, trains in under two minutes on a single GPU, and requires no LLM-based indexing.

</details>


### [368] [KGiRAG: An Iterative GraphRAG Approach for Responding Sensemaking Queries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20859)
*Isabela Iacob,Melisa Marian,Gheorghe Cosmin Silaghi*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces GraphRAG, a feedback-driven, iterative approach for answering complex queries using LLMs within graph-based RAG pipelines. It refines responses iteratively to enhance quality and relevance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome limitations in LLMs like hallucination and inability to handle large contexts that are critical for answering complex queries effectively.

Method: The authors developed an iterative GraphRAG architecture with feedback-driven response quality assessment to refine and improve query responses step-by-step.

Result: Using the HotPotQA dataset, the iterative GraphRAG approach showed higher semantic quality and relevance in responses compared to a traditional single-shot baseline.

Conclusion: The proposed iterative GraphRAG architecture improves LLM-based RAG pipelines by utilizing an iterative process and feedback loops to produce well-grounded, higher-quality responses.

Abstract: Recent literature highlights the potential of graph-based approaches within large language model (LLM) retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipelines for answering queries of varying complexity, particularly those that fall outside the LLM's prior knowledge. However, LLMs are prone to hallucination and often face technical limitations in handling contexts large enough to ground complex queries effectively. To address these challenges, we propose a novel iterative, feedback-driven GraphRAG architecture that leverages response quality assessment to iteratively refine outputs until a sound, well-grounded response is produced. Evaluating our approach with queries from the HotPotQA dataset, we demonstrate that this iterative RAG strategy yields responses with higher semantic quality and improved relevance compared to a single-shot baseline.

</details>


### [369] [Robust Test-time Video-Text Retrieval: Benchmarking and Adapting for Query Shifts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.20851)
*Bingqing Zhang,Zhuo Cao,Heming Du,Yang Li,Xue Li,Jiajun Liu,Sen Wang*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper identifies the vulnerability of video-text retrieval (VTR) models to query shifts and proposes HAT-VTR, a robustness framework to counteract this issue, achieving superior results in addressing query shift scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VTR models suffer significant performance drops under real-world query shifts. This paper seeks to address the need for robustness by developing solutions tailored to spatio-temporal complexities in video data, which are inadequately addressed by existing methods.

Method: The authors introduce a benchmark to assess query shifts via 12 perturbation types and propose HAT-VTR, which comprises a Hubness Suppression Memory for refining similarity scores and multi-granular losses for enhancing temporal feature alignment.

Result: Experiments show HAT-VTR significantly improves VTR robustness, outperforming existing techniques in handling diverse query shifts and proving its applicability to real-world scenarios.

Conclusion: HAT-VTR is an effective solution for making VTR models more reliable and robust under query shifts, addressing a significant gap in their real-world applicability.

Abstract: Modern video-text retrieval (VTR) models excel on in-distribution benchmarks but are highly vulnerable to real-world query shifts, where the distribution of query data deviates from the training domain, leading to a sharp performance drop. Existing image-focused robustness solutions are inadequate to handle this vulnerability in video, as they fail to address the complex spatio-temporal dynamics inherent in these shifts. To systematically evaluate this vulnerability, we first introduce a comprehensive benchmark featuring 12 distinct types of video perturbations across five severity degrees. Analysis on this benchmark reveals that query shifts amplify the hubness phenomenon, where a few gallery items become dominant "hubs" that attract a disproportionate number of queries. To mitigate this, we then propose HAT-VTR (Hubness Alleviation for Test-time Video-Text Retrieval), as our baseline test-time adaptation framework designed to directly counteract hubness in VTR. It leverages two key components: a Hubness Suppression Memory to refine similarity scores, and multi-granular losses to enforce temporal feature consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HAT-VTR substantially improves robustness, consistently outperforming prior methods across diverse query shift scenarios, and enhancing model reliability for real-world applications.

</details>
